output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.4, "R1.7": 0.7}}

Summary: Critique of the section, focusing on internal consistency, evidence-claim alignment, and unverified citations.

Substantive issues

1. Outcome variable vs. headline claim mismatch. The regression is described as having "correctly-identified-machine" as the binary outcome, which is a per-trial accuracy indicator. But the reported headline numbers (52.8%, 71.4%) are marginal accuracies. The text never reports the actual fitted model — odds ratios, the training effect coefficient, random-intercept variance, or how the p-value was derived. Either report the model output (e.g., "training raised log-odds of correct identification by β = …, p < .001") or drop the modeling description and present the comparison as a paired/within-subject test. As written, the model is mentioned but its results are not.

2. Design ambiguity: within- or between-subject training? "Mixed-design" is asserted, but it is unclear whether each of the 48 DBAs saw both untrained and trained blocks, or whether DBAs were randomized to a training arm. The two accuracy figures could come from the same raters pre/post, from independent groups, or from a crossover. This is the central inferential claim of the section and must be made explicit. If pre/post on the same raters, address practice/fatigue confounds (and whether a no-training control block was included). If between-subject, justify the comparability of arms.

3. Sample-size accounting. 48 raters × 60 pairs = 2,880 trials. A 95% CI half-width of ~3.5 points at 52.8% implies an effective n closer to ~800 independent observations once rater clustering is accounted for — which is plausible, but the CI construction should be stated (cluster-robust? from the mixed model? Wilson on raw counts?). Right now the CIs and the mixed-effects model are presented side by side with no link.

4. "Chance" baseline. Forced-choice 2AFC has a chance baseline of 50%. 52.8% with CI 49.1–56.5 includes 50, so "statistically indistinguishable from chance" is correct — but the lower bound being below 50 is worth a sentence: it means the untrained data cannot rule out slightly-below-chance performance, which is itself informative about whether DBAs have any latent signal pre-training.

5. Generalization claims overreach. The closing sentence claims "the first calibration of expert detection accuracy on a real-world OLTP workload." One production OLTP schema, queries from three organizations (likely overlapping in domain), and GPT-4 as the sole generator do not support a generalized "calibration." Soften to "a calibration on one production OLTP schema with GPT-4-generated queries," and note that detection accuracy is known to be model- and schema-specific.

6. Training protocol underspecified for a contribution claim. The contribution is framed as the training protocol itself, but the protocol is described only as "JOIN-ordering idioms and predicate-pushdown patterns characteristic of GPT-4 output." For this to be a contribution, the section needs either (a) the protocol as an artifact (appendix, materials) or (b) a description sufficient to replicate. Otherwise the contribution collapses to "training helped," which is unsurprising.

7. Selection of query pairs. "Held-out subset of a production OLTP schema" — held out from what? Was GPT-4 prompted zero-shot, few-shot, or with schema-linking? Were pairs matched on result-set equivalence, length, or complexity? Without this, the 52.8% baseline could reflect superficial cues (formatting, aliasing, comment style) rather than semantic SQL properties, which also undermines the meaning of the training gain.

8. Connection to prior work is thin. The opening paragraph cites Patel et al. and Ramirez & Choi but the study itself does not build on their instruments — no reuse of their Likert scales, no reuse of their κ methodology, no comparison of the trained-DBA accuracy to their naturalness-rating reliability. Either tighten the bridge ("we adopt Ramirez & Choi's pair-discrimination task and extend it with a training manipulation") or move those citations to a related-work discussion where the lack of methodological inheritance is less jarring.

9. Likely citation issues. "Patel et al." and "Ramirez and Choi" on DBA naturalness ratings and inter-annotator agreement on SQL naturalness — I could not verify these references and they do not match papers I can identify with confidence. The κ ≈ 0.41 figure attributed to Ramirez & Choi should be checked against the actual source; similar Likert-naturalness work on Text-to-SQL exists (e.g., around BIRD and Spider-Realistic) but I cannot confirm these specific author attributions.

10. Minor prose. "treating correctly-identified-machine as the binary outcome" reads awkwardly; prefer "with correct identification of the machine-generated query as the binary outcome." "Lifts expert ability … from chance to 71%" is loose — at 71.4% they are well above chance but still wrong nearly 30% of the time; "from chance to 71% accuracy (still ~29% error)" or similar would be more honest.

Recommended minimum revisions
- State the design (within/between/crossover) and the training-effect estimate from the fitted model.
- Specify the CI construction and how it relates to the mixed-effects model.
- Either include the training protocol as a referenceable artifact or reframe the contribution.
- Soften the "first calibration … on real-world OLTP workload" claim to the scope actually tested.
- Verify the Patel et al. and Ramirez & Choi citations and the κ ≈ 0.41 value.

Uncertainty disclosure: The two cited works (Patel et al.; Ramirez & Choi) and the κ ≈ 0.41 figure are unverified — I flagged them as needing a source check rather than confirming they are wrong. Statistical critique assumes a standard 2AFC paired-comparison design; if the actual design differs, items 1–4 should be re-evaluated against the true protocol.
